model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Duke is the #1 overall team in the country with a staggering +39.17 adjusted efficiency margin, featuring the nation's best defense (AdjD 88.7) that should neutralize Ohio State's strong but not elite offense (AdjO 124.4, 17th nationally), while Duke's own elite offense (AdjO 127.9) should exploit Ohio State's average defense (AdjD 102.1, 46th). The roughly 17-point efficiency margin gap per 100 possessions is enormous even for a 1-vs-8 matchup, and Cooper Flagg's successful return from his ankle injury in the Round of 64 rout removes the primary uncertainty around Duke's roster. Ohio State's Big Ten battle-testing and offensive firepower led by Bruce Thornton give them more upset potential than a typical 8-seed, but the quality gap is simply too wide for the Buckeyes to overcome against a historically dominant Duke team.

reasoningStep.title: East Round of 32: #1 Duke vs #8 Ohio State
reasoningStep.summary: Duke's historically elite efficiency profile—#1 in both KenPom and Torvik with the nation's best defense—creates an insurmountable gap against Ohio State despite the Buckeyes' respectable offensive metrics. The ~17-point adjusted efficiency margin difference and Flagg's healthy return make Duke a very strong favorite.
evidence: Duke Torvik #1 (AdjOE 128.0, AdjDE 90.4); Ohio State Torvik #23 (AdjOE 125.3, AdjDE 101.6)
Cooper Flagg returned from ankle injury and played well in Round of 64 rout
Ohio State dealt with injuries all season (Noel, Tilly, Royal) but getting healthier for postseason
1-seeds historically beat 8/9 seeds ~80% of the time; this efficiency gap is wider than typical 1-vs-8 matchup

result.text: Could Ohio State basketball get back to full health before postseason? Skip to main content Home Schedule NFL News Draft Fantasy Odds Betting Newsletter USA TODAY Sports As Ohio State gets healthier, coach Jake Diebler hoping for late push Adam Jardy Columbus Dispatch It’s been nearly two months since the Ohio State men’s basketball team started and finished a game with all its primary rotation players available. On Jan. 2, the Buckeyes resumed Big Ten play with an 80-73 road win against Rutgers. Sixth-year Wright State transfer Brandon Noel came off the bench to play 20 minutes in relief of freshman Amare Bynum, who teamed with Devin Royal, John Mobley Jr., Christoph Tilly and, of course, ever-reliable Bruce Thornton in the starting lineup. Three days later, Noel went down with a fracture in his left foot during a home loss to No. 10 Nebraska. He’s yet to return to action. Since that date, injuries and sickness have sidelined Tilly, Royal and Mobley at various points, sometimes forcing coach Jake Diebler to make last-minute lineup adjustments. The end of the season is on the horizon, but so is the potential of a healthy roster. With four games left before the Big Ten Tournament and the Buckeyes still with work to do to make the NCAA Tournament, Diebler said he increasingly believes Ohio State will dress a full roster before the postseason begins. “I’m hopeful, prayerful that we’re going to turn the corner on some of this stuff here really, really soon,” he said. “And if we do that, and get everybody available and moving in the same direction, watch out. I really believe that.” More immediately, that would mean the returns of both Mobley and Royal to the lineup. The former has missed three straight games with what Diebler confirmed is a finger injury suffered in a home win against USC on Feb. 11. The injury required a surgery, and Mobley was nearly cleared to play at No. 15 Michigan State on Feb. 22 but instead had to watch from the bench. Diebler said he’s “getting better and better each day” as the Buckeyes prepare to play at Iowa on Feb. 25. “With this injury there was never this specific date, there was this threshold that has to be met,” he said. “It’s a day-to-day thing for him, but there’s been progress. He is working so hard to get back as quickly as he can.” Royal joined Mobley and Noel on the bench inside the Breslin Center after coming down with an illness that had kept Bynum out of practice the day before the game. The freshman was able to battle through it and start against the Spartans, but Royal did not play after looking labored while going through warmups. Had the game been played Feb. 21, Royal would have played and Bynum would not have. “He tried to warm up and tried to see if he could give something to the team during the game,” Diebler said of Royal. “That conversation between him and our training staff ultimately is the final decision but you can’t sit there and say the kid didn’t try. He went out there, worked up a sweat an

Now I have comprehensive data. Let me compile my analysis.

**Key findings:**

**Duke (1-seed):**
- KenPom: #1 (AdjEM +39.17, AdjO 127.9 #4, AdjD 88.7 #1)
- Torvik: #1 (AdjOE 128.0 #4, AdjDE 90.4 #1)
- Record: 31-2
- Cooper Flagg returned from ankle injury and played well in Round of 64 rout

**Ohio State (8-seed):**
- KenPom: #26 (AdjEM +22.28, AdjO 124.4 #17, AdjD 102.1)
- Torvik: #23 (AdjOE 125.3 #12, AdjDE 101.6 #46)
- Record: 21-12
- Dealt with injuries all season (Noel, Tilly, Royal, Mobley) but getting healthier
- Bruce Thornton is their star, strong offense

**Efficiency gap:** Duke's AdjEM of +39.17 vs Ohio State's +22.28 = ~17 point gap per 100 possessions. Duke's defense (#1 nationally at 88.7) vs Ohio State's offense (#17 at 124.4) is the key matchup - even Ohio State's strong offense will be severely tested by the nation's best defense.

Ohio State has a respectable offense but their defense (102.1) is merely average, and Duke's elite offense (127.9) should exploit that. The gap is massive across every dimension.

Historically, 1-seeds beat 8/9 seeds roughly 80% of the time, and this particular 1-seed is the #1 overall team in the country.

The roughly 17-point efficiency margin gap per 100 possessions is enormous even for a 1-vs-8 matchup, and Cooper Flagg's successful return from his ankle injury in the Round of 64 rout removes the primary uncertainty around Duke's roster. Ohio State's Big Ten battle-testing and offensive firepower led by Bruce Thornton give them more upset potential than a typical 8-seed, but the quality gap is simply too wide for the Buckeyes to overcome against a historically dominant Duke team."
